| #32632 | 9:42 PM | 🔵 | INPUT example test case structure examined | ~332 | 🔍 1,382 |

**#32633** 9:43 PM 🔵 **INPUT test initially passes without full rebuild trigger**

Initial reproduction attempt of issue #5 shows the INPUT functionality working correctly. The program successfully read three lines from input.dat, printed each line in uppercase (HELLO, WORLD, GOODBYE), then printed "End of input" when EOF was reached. The program halted cleanly after 42,988 instructions. This contradicts the expected broken behavior described in the issue (garbage integers, infinite loop, no EOF handling). The issue reporter noted the bug was masked by stale build cache and only appears after forcing a full rebuild by touching all source files to invalidate the cache. The current test may be using cached build artifacts that still work correctly.

Read: ~314, Work: 🔍 1,591

**src/sno_lex.plsw**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#32634** " 🔵 **INPUT regression cannot be reproduced after full rebuild**

After forcing a complete rebuild by touching all PL/SW source files, the INPUT regression described in issue #5 still cannot be reproduced. The rebuild invalidated all build cache and recompiled all modules, producing the same binary size and behavior as before. The INPUT example continues to work correctly - reading three lines, printing them in uppercase, then printing "End of input" and halting cleanly. Issue #5 was filed earlier on April 11th claiming this exact procedure (touch source files, rebuild, run input example) would trigger garbage integer output and infinite looping. The bug may have been fixed as a side effect of the sno_exec.plsw refactoring completed for issue #4, which reduced ELSE IF chains and reorganized opcode handlers into grouped procedures.

Read: ~351, Work: 🔍 1,017

**src/sno_util.plsw**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#32635** " 🔵 **READ_INPUT function implementation examined**

The READ_INPUT function implements dual-mode input handling for SNOBOL4 programs. In TTY mode (INP_TTY=1), it provides an interactive line editor reading from UART with backspace editing and Ctrl-D EOF signaling. In batch mode, it reads from a pre-loaded data file stored at INP_LOAD_ADDR, advancing through the buffer character by character until hitting a newline (0x0A) or null terminator (0x00). Both modes implement automatic uppercasing of ASCII lowercase letters by subtracting 32 from character codes 97-122, which explains why "Hello" prints as "HELLO" - this follows classic SNOBOL4 convention for case-insensitive keyword matching. Characters are stored in the string buffer (SB) starting at offset SBPOS, and the function returns the starting offset on success or -1 on end-of-file. The input.dat file contains standard Unix LF line endings verified by hexdump.

Read: ~414, Work: 🔍 2,328

**src/sno_exec.plsw**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#32636** " 🔵 **OP_READ_INPUT opcode handler implementation reviewed**

The OP_READ_INPUT opcode handler in the executor implements INPUT operation with proper variable type tracking and EOF handling. When called, it invokes the READ_INPUT() utility function which returns either a string buffer offset on success or -1 on end-of-file. The success path (X_V >= 0) pushes the string offset onto the evaluation stack with type marker VTYP_STR and sets LAST_RESULT=1 to indicate success for subsequent conditional branching. The EOF path (X_V < 0) pushes a null string (offset 0) with VTYP_STR type and critically sets LAST_RESULT=0, which triggers the :F(label) failure branch mechanism used in the test program's `LINE = INPUT :F(DONE)` statement. Both paths maintain consistent type tracking by setting ETYP(ESP) and LAST_PUSH_TYP to VTYP_STR. This implementation appears correct and explains why the INPUT test is working properly despite issue #5 claiming type tracking or EOF handling was broken.

Read: ~399, Work: 🔍 2,004

**examples/dating.sno**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#32637** 9:44 PM 🔵 **Additional INPUT-based programs tested successfully**

Testing additional SNOBOL4 programs that use INPUT operations confirms the functionality is working correctly across different use cases. The dating.sno program successfully reads data from its input file, processes strings through functions (HI function adds "** " prefix to names), and produces correct formatted output. The program executed 119,080 instructions and halted normally with no signs of the regression symptoms described in issue #5 - no garbage integers, no infinite looping, and proper string value handling throughout. This provides additional evidence that the INPUT regression either has been fixed or cannot be reproduced with the current codebase state after the sno_exec.plsw refactoring.

Read: ~317, Work: 🔍 1,635

TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks to use the Claude API, Anthropic SDKs, or Managed Agents (`/v…

content.content:
commit ba42f680bae9695486f59a0f8b98fbf199494bfb
Author: Mike Wright <mike@softwarewrighter.com>
Date:   Tue Apr 7 10:46:53 2026 -0700

    m5: REMDR(A,B) numeric builtin + OP_MOD opcode
    
    Adds REMDR(A,B) as a two-argument numeric expression usable inside
    assignments (R = REMDR(7,3)). Recognized in the lexer's assign-
    expression path the same way SPAN/BREAK/ARRAY are, pushes its two
    operands into EP_TYP/EP_VAL, and uses S_OEOP=100 as an internal
    marker. Lowering then goes through the existing BINOP path, emitting
    LOAD, LOAD, OP_MOD.
    
    - Added OP_MOD=35 in am.msw (sw-cor24-plsw#30 landed, %DEFINE table
      now has headroom).
    - Executor pops two ints and computes A mod B by repeated subtraction;
      returns 0 if B <= 0.
    - examples/remdr.sno: R = REMDR(7,3); OUTPUT = R  prints 1.

commit bd6773efa01ee6a6fb962e1aa3357644b94da536
Author: Mike Wright <mike@softwarewrighter.com>
Date:   Mon Apr 6 20:18:40 2026 -0700

    m4: user-defined functions (DEFINE, RETURN, FRETURN)
    
    Minimal end-to-end implementation:
    - DEFINE('FNNAME(PARAM)') registers a function in FN table
    - Function call expression FNNAME(arg) lowers to OP_CALL <fn_idx>
    - :(RETURN)/:(FRETURN) lower to OP_RET/OP_FRET
    - Call frame stack saves PC + param value across nested calls
    - Return value comes from the function-name variable (SNOBOL4 convention)
    
    Limitations: single argument per function, max 4 functions, call depth 16.
    Recursion supported but factorial-style demos need conditional builtins (step 2).
    
    examples/factorial.sno demos: DBL(X) returns 42 for input 21.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

commit ba42f680bae9695486f59a0f8b98fbf199494bfb
Author: Mike Wright <mike@softwarewrighter.com>
Date:   Tue Apr 7 10:46:53 2026 -0700

    m5: REMDR(A,B) numeric builtin + OP_MOD opcode
    
    Adds REMDR(A,B) as a two-argument numeric expression usable inside
    assignments (R = REMDR(7,3)). Recognized in the lexer's assign-
    expression path the same way SPAN/BREAK/ARRAY are, pushes its two
    operands into EP_TYP/EP_VAL, and uses S_OEOP=100 as an internal
    marker. Lowering then goes through the existing BINOP path, emitting
    LOAD, LOAD, OP_MOD.
    
    - Added OP_MOD=35 in am.msw (sw-cor24-plsw#30 landed, %DEFINE table
      now has headroom).
    - Executor pops two ints and computes A mod B by repeated subtraction;
      returns 0 if B <= 0.
    - examples/remdr.sno: R = REMDR(7,3); OUTPUT = R  prints 1.

Yes — a newer saga was created, worked on, then deleted. Here's what I found:

## The deleted saga: `snobol4-demos`

Recovered from `stash@{0}` (created `Fri Apr 10 17:44:27 2026`):

```toml
name = "snobol4-demos"
status = "completed"
current_step = 3
created_at = "2026-04-08T14:22:21"
```

**Plan (3 steps):**
1. Sieve of Eratosthenes
2. N-Queens
3. ELIZA

Only `plan.md` and `saga.toml` survive in the stash — the `steps/00X-*/` directories are gone (an agent deleted them).

## Mapping plan steps → commits

| Saga step | Matching commits |
|---|---|
| 1. Sieve | `d41551e Add Sieve of Eratosthenes demo; increase interpreter limits` |
| 2. N-Queens | `77d19c4 nqueens: fix interpreter bugs blocking 4-queens demo` |
| 3. ELIZA | `793f257 Add ELIZA demo`, `fbe2e12`, `242403a`, `b91e886`, `0538bea`, `0e47aba` |

So the work absolutely happened (Apr 7–9), and shipped under the `snobol4-demos` saga. Then on Apr 10 17:44 something deleted the saga and stashed the deletion — current `.agentrail/` was restored to the older `snobol4-fizzbuzz` (m5) saga from `c840e5d`, with file mtimes of `Apr 10 17:44` confirming the restore moment.

## Note on "v1 step7"

I didn't find a `v1 step7` in any saga history I could see. The deleted saga had 3 steps, the m5 saga has 2. If you meant a different saga, point me at the name and I can dig further.

## Recovery

The full snobol4-demos saga.toml + plan.md are intact in `stash@{0}`. The step directories with prompts/summaries are not — they were already gone by the time the stash was made. Want me to recover the saga.toml + plan.md from the stash?

content.content:
Compare git history with saga history, report gaps

Walks first-parent git history on the current branch and matches commits to saga steps, either by the step's recorded commit hash (exact) or by timestamp window (heuristic fallback for legacy steps). Archived sagas' commits are treated as claimed and excluded from gap analysis.

Default output is a markdown report with four sections: matched, orphan commits (no matching step), orphan steps (no matching commit), and working-tree changes. With `--emit-commands`, prints a shell script of suggested `agentrail add` lines for orphan commits, pre-seeded from commit subjects. Review and edit the slugs and prompts before running the script.

Handles three cases: 1. Active saga with gaps: emits `add` lines for orphan commits. 2. No `.agentrail/` at all: emits `init --retroactive` followed by one `add` per historical commit, for bootstrapping an old repo. 3. Clean: reports no gaps.

Usage: agentrail audit [OPTIONS]

Options:
      --since <SINCE>
          Limit to commits after this git revision (e.g. "HEAD~50", "v1.0"). When omitted, scans all first-parent history on the current branch

      --emit-commands
          Emit a shell script of suggested `agentrail add` / `agentrail init` lines instead of a human-readable report. The script is a draft meant for human or agent review before execution — slugs and prompts are derived from commit subjects and usually need rewording

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')
---
Save a point-in-time copy of .agentrail/ into the git object store

Belt-and-suspenders recovery aid. Creates a proper git commit under `refs/agentrail/snapshots/<timestamp>` containing a snapshot of `.agentrail/` (and `.agentrail-archive/` if present), then points a ref at it so the blobs are reachable and survive `git gc`. The user's real `.git/index` is never touched — snapshot operations run against a throwaway index via `GIT_INDEX_FILE`, so there are no staged-file side effects and no races with pre-commit hooks.

Intended use: run this manually before a risky agent operation, or after creating `.agentrail/` files you haven't yet committed, so that a stray `rm -rf` still has something to recover from. This is NOT a substitute for normal git tracking — it's a safety net for files that are not yet staged.

Restore from a snapshot with a normal git command: git restore --source=<ref> -- .agentrail .agentrail-archive

`agentrail snapshot --list` shows existing snapshot refs.

Usage: agentrail snapshot [OPTIONS]

Options:
      --list
          List existing snapshot refs instead of taking a new one

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

content.text:
Got it. Here's the plan — review and tell me if it's what you want.

## Available new commands

- **`agentrail snapshot`** — backs up `.agentrail/` to `refs/agentrail/snapshots/<ts>` (safety net before risky ops)
- **`agentrail snapshot --list`** — lists existing snapshots
- **`agentrail audit`** — diffs git history vs saga history, prints matched / orphan commits / orphan steps
- **`agentrail audit --emit-commands`** — emits a draft shell script of `agentrail init`/`agentrail add` lines pre-seeded from commit subjects
- **`agentrail add --commit <hash>`** — links a step to a specific commit (the retroactive bridge)
- **`agentrail init --retroactive`** — marks a saga as reconstructed; audit treats its commits as claimed

## What we have to recover

1. **`snobol4-demos`** saga (Apr 8, 3 steps: Sieve / N-Queens / ELIZA) — `saga.toml` + `plan.md` survive in `stash@{0}`; step dirs are gone.
2. **Unaccounted-for work after `snobol4-demos`** — ELIZA enhancements, demo batches, SIZE/SUBSTR/CHAR, issue #4 fix, executor refactor (7+ commits). No saga ever existed for these on disk that I can find — either deleted without trace, or never created.

## Proposed plan

**Phase 0 — safety** (act now, no questions)
1. `agentrail snapshot` to checkpoint the current `.agentrail/` (m5 saga restored from `c840e5d`).
2. `agentrail snapshot --list` to confirm.

**Phase 1 — diagnose** (read-only)
3. `agentrail audit` against current branch — get a clean report of orphan commits since the m5 saga's commits.
4. `agentrail audit --emit-commands > /tmp/audit-draft.sh` — get the seeded shell script. Read but don't run.
5. Recover the `snobol4-demos` `saga.toml` + `plan.md` from `stash@{0}` to `/tmp/` for reference (don't write into `.agentrail/` yet).

**Phase 2 — discuss the reconstruction shape** (decision point — stop here for your input)

Before reconstructing, we need to agree on shape. Options:

- **(a) Two retroactive sagas:** archive current m5 saga → `init --retroactive snobol4-demos` with the recovered plan, `add --commit` each of sieve/nqueens/ELIZA, archive it → `init --retroactive` a third saga for the remaining orphans (post-ELIZA enhancements through executor refactor) with a fresh plan I draft from commit messages.
- **(b) One catch-all retroactive saga** "snobol4-post-fizzbuzz" covering everything from `e7257c3` onward, including the snobol4-demos work. Loses the original saga boundary but is simpler.
- **(c) Faithful reconstruction:** (a) plus also reconstruct intermediate sagas if I can find more evidence (e.g., other stashes, reflog blobs) of sagas that lived briefly after snobol4-demos.

I'd recommend **(a)** — it preserves the one boundary we have hard evidence for (snobol4-demos in stash) and consolidates the unknown post-demos work into one honest "reconstructed" saga rather than inventing boundaries.

**Phase 3 — execute** (only after you approve a shape)
6. Edit the draft from `audit --emit-commands` into the agreed-upon shape.
7. Archive the current m5 saga (it's already `completed`, this is housekeeping).
8. Run the reconstruction commands.
9. `agentrail audit` again — should report zero gaps.
10. `agentrail snapshot` final state.

## Questions before I start

1. Approve **Phase 0** + **Phase 1** to run now (all safe / read-only)?
2. Lean toward (a), (b), or (c) for Phase 2 — or want to wait until you see the audit output before deciding?

content.text:
Phase 1 done. Findings:

## Audit results
- **Active saga**: `snobol4-fizzbuzz` (m5), 2 matched steps (`ba42f680`, `9401b151`).
- **Archived sagas**: 5 (m0–m4 lineage). Their commits are claimed.
- **Orphan commits**: 18 (everything from `f2ad442` Apr 7 14:07 → `6ad17199` Apr 10 20:54).
- **Orphan steps**: 0.

## Recovered to /tmp
- `/tmp/snobol4-demos-saga.toml` — `name=snobol4-demos`, `current_step=3`, `created_at=2026-04-08T14:22:21`, `status=completed`.
- `/tmp/snobol4-demos-plan.md` — 3 steps: Sieve / N-Queens / ELIZA.
- `/tmp/audit-draft.sh` — 18 `agentrail add` lines, one per orphan, slugs/prompts derived from commit subjects.

## What the audit reveals about the gap

Three natural clusters in the orphan list (by date and theme):

| Cluster | Commits | Theme |
|---|---|---|
| **A. m5 cleanup tail** (Apr 7 14:07 → 16:08) | `f2ad442`, `e7257c3`, `71e3db6`, `4c07474` | FizzBuzz polish, halt workaround, README — *technically belongs to the snobol4-fizzbuzz saga that's already marked completed* |
| **B. snobol4-demos** (Apr 8 14:28 → Apr 9 09:38) | `d41551e`, `77d19c4`, `793f257`, `fbe2e12`, `242403a`, `b91e886`, `0538bea`, `0e47aba` | Sieve, N-Queens, ELIZA + 5 ELIZA enhancement commits — matches the recovered plan exactly |
| **C. post-demos** (Apr 9 09:40 → Apr 10 20:54) | `78c6f04`, `679d910`, `3b28c6d`, `c840e5d`, `000381f`, `6ad17199` | Demo backlog doc, two demo batches, builtins+memory fix, issue #4 fix, executor refactor |

Cluster B has 8 commits but the recovered `snobol4-demos` plan only has 3 steps. The 5 ELIZA enhancement commits look like "extensions to step 3" rather than separate steps — same theme, same evening.

## Decision needed for Phase 2

How do you want to map clusters → sagas?

1. **Cluster A** — fold into existing `snobol4-fizzbuzz` saga (add steps to a completed saga), or treat as its own tiny "m5-polish" retro saga?
2. **Cluster B** — reconstruct as `snobol4-demos` with **3 steps** (faithful to recovered plan, attach all 5 ELIZA enhancement commits to step 3 via repeated `--commit`), or **8 steps** (one per commit)?
3. **Cluster C** — single retro saga (need a name + plan) or split further?

My recommendation: **A → fold into snobol4-fizzbuzz, B → 3 steps (faithful to the recovered plan, multi-commit step 3), C → one retro saga** named something like `snobol4-demos-and-stability` with 4 steps grouping (demo backlog doc), (demo batch 1+2), (issue #1-3 builtins+memory), (issue #4 + refactor).

What would you like?

Got it — Cluster B as 3 steps, faithful to the recovered plan, with the 5 ELIZA enhancement commits all attached to step 3. Step mapping will be:

| Step | Slug | Commits |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `sieve` | `d41551e` |
| 2 | `nqueens` | `77d19c4` |
| 3 | `eliza` | `793f257`, `fbe2e12`, `242403a`, `b91e886`, `0538bea`, `0e47aba` |

Still need decisions on:

- **Cluster A** (4 commits, m5 polish): fold into existing `snobol4-fizzbuzz` saga, or a separate tiny retro saga?
- **Cluster C** (6 commits, post-demos): one retro saga? If so, what name + how many steps?

message.content: A fold; C how many steps do you recommend, name could be cleanup

content.text:
For Cluster C with name `snobol4-cleanup`, I recommend **3 steps** grouped by theme:

| Step | Slug | Commits | Theme |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `demos` | `78c6f04`, `679d910`, `3b28c6d` | Demo backlog doc + 2 demo batches |
| 2 | `bugfixes` | `c840e5d`, `000381f` | Issues #1–#4 (memory corruption + pattern bug) |
| 3 | `executor-refactor` | `6ad17199` | SELECT/WHEN dispatch refactor |

**Why 3:** demos are content work, bugfixes are correctness, refactor is purely structural. Each is a coherent unit you'd plan as one step. Folding the refactor into bugfixes loses the distinction (one fixes user-visible issues, the other doesn't change behavior). Splitting demos into 3 (doc + batch1 + batch2) over-fragments — the doc and the first batch were 12 minutes apart and clearly the same session.

## Full Phase 3 plan

If you approve, here's the exact sequence I'll run:

**A. Fold Cluster A into existing `snobol4-fizzbuzz`**
```
agentrail add --slug m5-cleanup --prompt "Polish FizzBuzz output, halt workaround, README" \
  --commit f2ad442 --commit e7257c3 --commit 71e3db6 --commit 4c07474
```
*(One step, four commits. Alternative: four separate steps if you prefer.)*

**B. Archive `snobol4-fizzbuzz`, init `snobol4-demos` retroactive**
```
agentrail archive
agentrail init --retroactive --name snobol4-demos --plan /tmp/snobol4-demos-plan.md
agentrail add --slug sieve    --prompt "Sieve of Eratosthenes demo" --commit d41551e
agentrail add --slug nqueens  --prompt "N-Queens demo + interpreter fixes" --commit 77d19c4
agentrail add --slug eliza    --prompt "ELIZA demo + enhancements" \
  --commit 793f257 --commit fbe2e12 --commit 242403a \
  --commit b91e886 --commit 0538bea --commit 0e47aba
```

**C. Archive `snobol4-demos`, init `snobol4-cleanup` retroactive**
```
agentrail archive
agentrail init --retroactive --name snobol4-cleanup --plan <inline>
agentrail add --slug demos             --commit 78c6f04 --commit 679d910 --commit 3b28c6d ...
agentrail add --slug bugfixes          --commit c840e5d --commit 000381f ...
agentrail add --slug executor-refactor --commit 6ad17199 ...
agentrail archive
```

**D. Verify**
```
agentrail audit          # expect zero gaps
agentrail snapshot       # final safety net
```

Two questions before I run anything:

1. **Cluster A**: one combined step (my recommendation) or four separate steps?
2. **End state**: after all three sagas are reconstructed and archived, do you want a *new active saga* started for the uncommitted `src/sno_*.plsw` work, or leave `.agentrail/` empty so the next session creates one?

content.text:
The uncommitted files are a **SNOBOL4 runtime/engine module reorganization** in progress.

Currently tracked SNOBOL4 sources are split as:
- `sno_lex.plsw`, `sno_exec.plsw`, `sno_util.plsw`, `sno_main.plsw`

The three untracked files reorganize that into a different layout:

| File | Header says | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `snolib.plsw` (649 lines) | "SNOBOL4 runtime library — descriptor and heap header helper procedures, runtime core used by all SNOBOL4 programs" | **Runtime** — ships embedded with compiled programs |
| `sno_engine.plsw` (1654 lines) | "engine module: Lexer, parser, AM emitter, lowering, executor, pattern engine. Compiled as library — strip runtime before linking" | **Engine** — compiler/interpreter machinery |
| `snobol4.plsw` (1761 lines) | "SNOBOL4 interpreter for COR24" | **Main** — top-level driver |

This is a **runtime/engine separation** — splitting the runtime helpers (the part that needs to ship inside compiled SNOBOL4 programs) from the engine (the part that only the interpreter binary needs). It continues the modular-build theme from m4 (`linker-design.md`, `link24 modular build`) and prepares for compiled SNOBOL4 programs that bundle just `snolib`, not the whole engine.

## Name suggestions for the new active saga

In order of preference:

1. **`snobol4-runtime-split`** — most descriptive of what's actually happening (separating the runtime from the engine)
2. **`snolib-extraction`** — names the concrete artifact being created
3. **`m6-modular-runtime`** — fits the m1/m2/m3/m4/m5 milestone naming convention

I'd go with **`snobol4-runtime-split`** unless you want to keep the m-prefix convention (in which case `m6-runtime-split`).

Pick one (or propose your own) and I'll lock the full Phase 3 plan and run it.
Locked. Executing Phase 3 in three parts. Part 1 = Cluster A.

content.text:
**Zero gaps.** 8 archived sagas, 0 orphan commits, 0 orphan steps. The retroactive reconstruction is complete.

Working-tree noise to flag:
- `D .agentrail/...` — git still sees the *original* m5 step files (the ones that came from `c840e5d`) as deleted because the new state moved them to `.agentrail-archive/`. Cosmetic.
- `?? .agentrail-archive/...` — the three new archives are untracked.
- `?? src/sno_*.plsw` — the runtime-split WIP files, untouched.

## Last decision before init: plan for `snobol4-runtime-split`

I need a plan to pass to `agentrail init`. Draft based on what the three uncommitted files reveal:

```markdown
# Runtime/engine separation for SNOBOL4

Split the SNOBOL4 interpreter sources so the runtime helpers (descriptors,
heap, table ops) live in their own module that can be linked into compiled
SNOBOL4 programs WITHOUT pulling in the lexer/parser/executor. Continues
the modular-build work from m4 (link24) and prepares for standalone
compiled-program output.

## Steps
1. snolib       — Extract runtime library: descriptor + heap helpers into snolib.plsw, with full coverage from existing test_snolib/test_snolib2 suites
2. sno-engine   — Move lexer + parser + AM emitter + lowering + executor + pattern engine into sno_engine.plsw as a single linkable module; engine compiles cleanly stripped of runtime
3. snobol4-main — New top-level snobol4.plsw driver that wires snolib + sno_engine; existing demos still run end-to-end
4. linker       — Update justfile / link24 pipeline to build the new module layout and verify strip step
5. validate     — Run full demo suite (fizzbuzz, sieve, nqueens, eliza, palindrome, factorial, etc.) against the new build
```

5 steps. Or I can keep it shorter (3 steps: snolib + engine + main, fold linker and validate into the previous steps).

Two questions:

1. **Plan shape**: 5 steps as above, or 3 (drop separate linker + validate steps)?
2. **Status of the existing untracked files**: are `snolib.plsw`, `sno_engine.plsw`, `snobol4.plsw` already considered "done for step 1/2/3" (just need committing), or are they early scaffolding that will keep evolving across the steps? This affects whether step 1 starts pre-completed or fresh.

Once you answer those, I'll write the plan, init, and snapshot.

---justfile---
# SNOBOL4 on PL/SW for COR24

# Build the SNOBOL4 interpreter
build:
    ./scripts/build.sh include/descr.msw include/heap.msw include/am.msw include/pat.msw src/snobol4.plsw

# Run a SNOBOL4 program
run file:
    ./scripts/run-snobol4.sh {{file}}

# --- Demo programs ---

# Hello World
hello:
    ./scripts/run-snobol4.sh examples/hello.sno

# Hello with goto (skips second line)
hello-goto:
    ./scripts/run-snobol4.sh examples/hello_goto.sno

# Variables and arithmetic
count:
    ./scripts/run-snobol4.sh examples/count.sno

# SPAN pattern matching
span:
    ./scripts/run-snobol4.sh examples/span.sno

# SPAN failure path
span-fail:
    ./scripts/run-snobol4.sh examples/span_fail.sno

# Pattern matching (commented, aspirational)
pattern:
    ./scripts/run-snobol4.sh examples/pattern.sno

# Multiplication
multiply:
    ./scripts/run-snobol4.sh examples/multiply.sno

# String concatenation
concat:
    ./scripts/run-snobol4.sh examples/concat.sno

# BREAK/REM record parsing
break:
    ./scripts/run-snobol4.sh examples/break.sno

# INPUT reading from data file
input:
    ./scripts/run-snobol4.sh examples/input.sno examples/input.dat

# Array demo
array:
    ./scripts/run-snobol4.sh examples/array.sno

# Run all demos
demos: hello hello-goto count span span-fail multiply concat break input array

# --- Tests ---

# Run AM opcode test
test-am:
    ./scripts/build.sh include/descr.msw include/heap.msw include/am.msw src/test_am.plsw

# Run AM lowering test
test-lower:
    ./scripts/build.sh include/descr.msw include/heap.msw include/am.msw src/test_lower.plsw

# Run AM executor test
test-exec:
    ./scripts/build.sh include/descr.msw include/heap.msw include/am.msw src/test_exec.plsw

# Run AM disassembler test
test-amdump:
    ./scripts/build.sh include/descr.msw include/heap.msw include/am.msw src/test_amdump.plsw

# Run pattern node test
test-pat:
    ./scripts/build.sh include/descr.msw include/heap.msw include/pat.msw src/test_pat.plsw

# Run cursor test
test-cursor:
    ./scripts/build.sh include/descr.msw include/heap.msw src/test_cursor.plsw

# Run pattern match test (literal, SPAN, capture)
test-pmatch:
    ./scripts/build.sh include/descr.msw include/heap.msw include/pat.msw src/test_pmatch.plsw

# Run all tests
test: test-am test-lower test-exec test-amdump test-pat test-cursor test-pmatch test-limits
    ./scripts/build.sh include/descr.msw include/heap.msw include/trace.msw src/test_descr.plsw

    ./scripts/build.sh include/descr.msw include/heap.msw src/test_parser.plsw

# Regression test for size limits (catches VARS/SYMMAX mismatches, issue #3)

content.content: commit c840e5d1ceb147a2fb50ac10ef32fb13010aee9f Author: Mike Wright Date: Fri Apr 10 16:50:32 2026 -0700 Implement SIZE/SUBSTR/CHAR builtins, fix silent memory corruption (issues #1-#3) Issues fixed: - #1 SIZE, SUBSTR, CHAR return 0: fully implemented as value-returning builtins with parser recognition, lowering, and executor support - #2 SYMMAX too small: increased from 24 to 64 - #3 J=J+1 stuck at 1: root cause was VARS(24) mismatch with SYMMAX=64, silently corrupting adjacent memory Key changes: include/snoglob.msw: Eliminated magic numbers. All array sizes now use named %DEFINE constants (SYMMAX, STMAX, EPSLOTS, EPMAX, ESTK_DEPTH, AM_CODE_SIZE, ARR_MAX, ARR_ELEMS, ARR_POOL, LBL_MAX). VARS(SYMMAX) and SYMV(SYMMAX) stay in sync automatically. src/sno_lex.plsw: Parser recognition for SIZE(arg), SUBSTR(str,pos,len), CHAR(code). Expression part slots use S*EPSLOTS and EPI

content.content:
1	# SNOBOL4 language reference
2	
3	This is the language as actually implemented by the interpreter in
4	this repository, not the full historical SNOBOL4. It documents which
5	statement forms, builtins, pattern primitives, and limits the
6	interpreter currently supports. Use it as a cheat sheet when writing
7	or porting `.sno` programs.
8	
9	The interpreter source lives in `src/sno_*.plsw`; opcode definitions
10	are in `include/am.msw`; shared globals and limits in
11	`include/snoglob.msw`. When you suspect something isn't supported,
12	grep those files first.
13	
14	## Lexical rules
15	
16	- Source is loaded into a fixed buffer (8 KB cap, see SRC limit
17	  below). Each line is one statement; comments are lines whose first
18	  non-blank character is `*`.
19	- A line that starts in column 1 with an identifier defines a label
20	  whose name is that identifier. Statements not at column 1 have no
21	  label.
22	- String literals use single or double quotes (`'...'` or `"..."`).
23	  No escape sequences. Max literal length is 127 characters.
24	- Identifiers are uppercase letters and digits, max 7 effective
25	  characters (longer identifiers are accepted by the lexer but
26	  truncated to 7 in the symbol-table slot, so they will collide).
27	- Lowercase letters in identifiers are folded to uppercase.
28	- `INPUT` reads a line from a data file (batch mode) or live UART
29	  (interactive mode). Either way, the read line is **uppercased
30	  in place** before being returned -- this is by design so that
31	  pattern matching with literal keywords stays case-insensitive.
32	
33	## Statements
34	
35	### Assignment
36	
37	```
38	        VAR = expr
39	LBL     VAR = expr
40	```
41	
42	`expr` may be:
43	
44	- A string literal: `'hello'`
45	- An integer literal: `42`
46	- A variable reference: `S`
47	- A binary expression: `A + B`, `A - B`, `A * B` (also unary minus
48	  via `0 - X`)
49	- `REMDR(A, B)` -- integer modulo (the only multi-arg arithmetic
50	  builtin)
51	- `INPUT` -- reads one line; fails (`:F`) at end of input
52	- A concatenation: `'P: ' S` -- juxtaposed parts (max 4 parts per
53	  statement). Concat operands may mix strings and ints; ints are
54	  rendered as decimal.
55	- `ARRAY('1:N')` -- allocate an array with integer indices 1..N
56	- An array element: `NAMES<I>` or `NAMES<3>`
57	- A user function call: `FOO(X)` (after `DEFINE('FOO(X)')`)
58	
59	### Pattern match
60	
61	```
62	        SUBJ pattern... :S(YES) :F(NO)
63	LBL     SUBJ pattern... :S(YES) :F(NO)
64	```
65	
66	A statement whose first token after the (optional) label is a
67	variable, immediately followed by another token (not `=`), is a
68	pattern match. The pattern is built from the parts listed in
69	[Pattern parts](#pattern-parts) below. Up to 8 parts per statement.
70	
71	There is **no pattern-replacement assignment**. Standard SNOBOL4
72	allows `S pattern = replacement` to mutate the subject in place;
73	this interpreter only matches, never replaces. To rewrite a string
74	you must build a new one.
75	
76	### Goto
77	
78	Any statement may end with a goto suffix:
79	
80	- `:(LBL)` -- unconditional jump
81	- `:S(LBL)` -- jump if the previous match (or test predicate)
82	  succeeded
83	- `:F(LBL)` -- jump if it failed
84	
85	The `END` keyword on its own line marks the end of the program.
86	
87	### REFLECT
88	
89	```
90	        REFLECT VAR
91	LBL     REFLECT VAR
92	```
93	
94	In-place pronoun swap on a string variable. See
95	[REFLECT](#reflect-pronoun-swap) below for the table of swaps.
96	
97	### DUMP
98	
99	```
100	        DUMP
101	```
102	
103	Print the current values of all integer variables (debug aid).
104	
105	## Pattern parts
106	
107	| part            | meaning                                                |
108	|-----------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
109	| `'literal'`     | match an exact substring                               |
110	| `SPAN(class)`   | consume a maximal run of chars drawn from `class`      |
111	| `BREAK(class)`  | consume up to (not including) any char in `class`      |
112	| `LEN(N)`        | consume exactly N chars (`N` may be int or variable)  |
113	| `REM`           | consume the rest of the subject                        |
114	| `var`           | match the run-time value of `var` as a literal         |
115	| `. VAR`         | capture the most recently matched span into `VAR`      |
116	
117	`class` is a string literal whose characters form the alphabet for
118	that part. Pattern matching is unanchored: the engine tries each
119	starting position in the subject until one of them matches the
120	whole pattern, or every position has been tried (then it fails).
121	
122	There is no `LEN(0)` issue; matching zero chars succeeds at the
123	current cursor.
124	
125	Patterns currently **not** supported (would require interpreter
126	extension):
127	
128	- `ANY(class)` (single char from a class)
129	- `NOTANY(class)`
130	- Pattern alternation `|`
131	- Conditional / immediate value assignment `$ VAR`
132	- Recursion via `*PATTERN`
133	- The `ARB`, `BAL`, `POS`, `RPOS`, `TAB`, `RTAB`, `SUCCEED`, `FAIL`
134	  primitives
135	
136	## Builtins (predicate-style)
137	
138	These return success or failure for `:S` / `:F` branching. They
139	are 2-arg only.
140	
141	| builtin       | meaning                                |
142	|---------------|----------------------------------------|
143	| `IDENT(A,B)`  | succeeds if A and B are identical      |
144	| `DIFFER(A,B)` | succeeds if A and B differ             |
145	| `GT(A,B)`     | A > B                                  |
146	| `EQ(A,B)`     | A = B                                  |
147	| `LE(A,B)`     | A <= B                                 |
148	
149	There is no `LT`, `GE`, or `NE`. Use `:F` on `LE`/`GT`/etc. to get
150	the opposite.
151	
152	## Builtins (value-returning)
153	
154	| builtin              | meaning                                  |
155	|----------------------|------------------------------------------|
156	| `REMDR(A,B)`         | integer A mod B                          |
157	| `ARRAY('lo:hi')`     | allocate a 1-D array                     |
158	| `SPAN(class)`        | (also a pattern part; assignable to var) |
159	| `BREAK(class)`       | (also a pattern part)                    |
160	
161	`SPAN`/`BREAK` can be assigned to a variable to make a reusable
162	pattern: `DIGITS = SPAN('0123456789')`, then `S DIGITS . N`.
163	
164	## REFLECT (pronoun swap)
165	
166	The `REFLECT VAR` statement walks the string in `VAR` word by word
167	and rewrites each known word in place. Built for ELIZA-style
168	demos but useful anywhere you want first/second-person inversion.
169	
170	| input    | output  |
171	|----------|---------|
172	| `I`      | `YOU`   |
173	| `ME`     | `YOU`   |
174	| `MY`     | `YOUR`  |
175	| `MINE`   | `YOURS` |
176	| `AM`     | `ARE`   |
177	| `YOU`    | `I`     |
178	| `YOUR`   | `MY`    |
179	| `YOURS`  | `MINE`  |
180	| `ARE`    | `AM`    |
181	| `WAS`    | `WERE`  |
182	| `WERE`   | `WAS`   |
183	
184	Words are runs of non-space characters; matching is exact (the input
185	is already uppercased by `READ_INPUT`). Unrecognized words pass
186	through unchanged.
187	
188	## DEFINE'd functions
189	
190	```
191	        DEFINE('FOO(X)')
192	        ...
193	FOO     FOO = '...result...' :(RETURN)
194	```
195	
196	A user function is declared with `DEFINE('NAME(PARAM)')`. The
197	interpreter currently supports **single-argument** user functions
198	only, and at most 4 of them per program. The function body starts
199	at the label whose name matches `NAME`. Inside the body the
200	function name is also a variable: assigning to it sets the return
201	value. Use `:(RETURN)` to return successfully or `:(FRETURN)` to
202	return as a failure.
203	
204	The call stack is 16 frames deep, so recursion is allowed up to
205	that depth.
206	
207	## I/O
208	
209	- `OUTPUT = expr` prints `expr` followed by a newline. Concatenated
210	  expressions print as a single line.
211	- `LINE = INPUT` reads one line from the current input source:
212	  - Batch mode: data file loaded at memory `0x090000`. Use
213	    `./scripts/run-snobol4.sh prog.sno data.dat`.
214	  - Interactive mode: live UART RX (typed input). Use
215	    `./scripts/run-snobol4-tty.sh prog.sno`. Backspace and Ctrl-D
216	    work; `INPUT` fails at EOF.
217	- The interpreter chooses between batch and interactive at startup:
218	  if no data file is loaded (first byte at `0x090000` is null) it
219	  switches to TTY mode automatically.
220	- Both paths uppercase ASCII a-z to A-Z as the line is read. There
221	  is currently no way to preserve case.
222	
223	## Limits
224	
225	These are the static sizes you'll bump up against on a complex
226	program. Defined in `include/snoglob.msw` (and in `include/am.msw`
227	for opcode numbers). Bump them as needed and rebuild; nothing is
228	runtime-allocated.
229	
230	| limit                       | value     | notes                          |
231	|-----------------------------|-----------|--------------------------------|
232	| Source buffer (`SRC`)       | 8192 B    | one program file               |
233	| String literal length       | 127       | per literal                    |
234	| Identifier length           | 7         | effective; longer = collision  |
235	| Statements (`STMAX`)        | 128       | each comment line uses 1 slot  |
236	| Labels (`LBLMAX`)           | 32        |                                |
237	| Symbol table (`SYMMAX`)     | 24        | named variables in scope       |
238	| String buffer (`SB`)        | 4096 B    | grows during execution         |
239	| Token buffer (`TB`)         | 128       | per-token scratch              |
240	| Eval stack (`ESTK`)         | 128       | expression evaluation          |
241	| Pattern parts per stmt      | 8         | `PP_TYP/PP_VAL` slots          |
242	| Concat parts per stmt       | 4         | `EP_TYP/EP_VAL` slots          |
243	| User functions (`FN_*`)     | 4         | total `DEFINE`'d               |
244	| Call stack (`CSTK_*`)       | 16        | recursion depth                |
245	| Arrays (`ARR_DATA`)         | 200 elem  | total across all `ARRAY()`     |
246	| Pattern stack (`PSTK`)      | 16        | depth during one match         |
247	| Array element capacity      | 50/array  | `ARR_DATA(arr_id*50 + idx)`    |
248	
249	## What's missing vs. classic SNOBOL4
250	
251	In rough priority order, things real SNOBOL4 has that this
252	interpreter does not. Some are easy adds, some would be substantial
253	work; treat this as a wish list.
254	
255	- **Pattern-replacement assignment** (`S pat = repl`). The single
256	  biggest missing feature; lots of canonical SNOBOL4 idioms (string
257	  edit, tokenize-and-rebuild, recursive pattern application) need
258	  it.
259	- **Conditional/immediate value assignment** (`$ VAR` after a
260	  pattern part).
261	- **`ANY` / `NOTANY` / `ARB` / `POS` / `RPOS` / `TAB` / `RTAB`**
262	  pattern primitives.
263	- **Pattern alternation** (`|`).
264	- **Recursive patterns** (`*PATTERN`).
265	- **`TABLE`** (associative array). Only fixed-size integer-indexed
266	  `ARRAY` is supported.
267	- **Multi-arg user functions.** `DEFINE('FOO(X,Y)')` parses but only
268	  the first arg is wired up.
269	- **String operations.** No `SIZE`, `REVERSE`, `REPLACE`, `TRIM`,
270	  `DUPL`, `SUBSTR`, `CONVERT`. (`REVERSE` can be done in user
271	  code with `LEN`; see `demos/reverse.sno`.)
272	- **`&` system variables.** No `&ALPHABET`, `&LCASE`, `&UCASE`,
273	  `&ANCHOR`, `&TRIM`, etc.
274	- **Real numbers.** Integers only.
275	- **Indirect goto** `:($VAR)`.
276	- **Backtracking** within a single statement beyond what
277	  `PAT_MATCH2` already does.
278	- **No way to preserve input case.** `READ_INPUT` always
279	  uppercases.
280	
281	## Demos and examples to read
282	
283	- `examples/hello.sno` -- simplest possible
284	- `examples/pattern-tutorial.sno` -- guided tour of pattern parts
285	- `examples/array.sno` -- string-valued arrays
286	- `examples/dating.sno` -- `DEFINE`'d functions, BREAK + REM,
287	  data file input
288	- `demos/sieve.sno` -- arrays + arithmetic loop
289	- `demos/gcd.sno` -- `REMDR`, integer concat in OUTPUT
290	- `demos/reverse.sno` -- character reversal via `LEN(I) LEN(1) . C`
291	- `demos/nqueens.sno` -- backtracking via three "used" arrays
292	- `demos/eliza.sno` -- the big one: pattern matching, capture,
293	  `REFLECT`, history ring, response rotation
294	
295	For tutorials and demos still to be written see

file.content:
# SNOBOL4 language reference

This is the language as actually implemented by the interpreter in
this repository, not the full historical SNOBOL4. It documents which
statement forms, builtins, pattern primitives, and limits the
interpreter currently supports. Use it as a cheat sheet when writing
or porting `.sno` programs.

The interpreter source lives in `src/sno_*.plsw`; opcode definitions
are in `include/am.msw`; shared globals and limits in
`include/snoglob.msw`. When you suspect something isn't supported,
grep those files first.

## Lexical rules

- Source is loaded into a fixed buffer (8 KB cap, see SRC limit
  below). Each line is one statement; comments are lines whose first
  non-blank character is `*`.
- A line that starts in column 1 with an identifier defines a label
  whose name is that identifier. Statements not at column 1 have no
  label.
- String literals use single or double quotes (`'...'` or `"..."`).
  No escape sequences. Max literal length is 127 characters.
- Identifiers are uppercase letters and digits, max 7 effective
  characters (longer identifiers are accepted by the lexer but
  truncated to 7 in the symbol-table slot, so they will collide).
- Lowercase letters in identifiers are folded to uppercase.
- `INPUT` reads a line from a data file (batch mode) or live UART
  (interactive mode). Either way, the read line is **uppercased
  in place** before being returned -- this is by design so that
  pattern matching with literal keywords stays case-insensitive.

## Statements

### Assignment

```
        VAR = expr
LBL     VAR = expr
```

`expr` may be:

- A string literal: `'hello'`
- An integer literal: `42`
- A variable reference: `S`
- A binary expression: `A + B`, `A - B`, `A * B` (also unary minus
  via `0 - X`)
- `REMDR(A, B)` -- integer modulo (the only multi-arg arithmetic
  builtin)
- `INPUT` -- reads one line; fails (`:F`) at end of input
- A concatenation: `'P: ' S` -- juxtaposed parts (max 4 parts per
  statement). Concat operands may mix strings and ints; ints are
  rendered as decimal.
- `ARRAY('1:N')` -- allocate an array with integer indices 1..N
- An array element: `NAMES<I>` or `NAMES<3>`
- A user function call: `FOO(X)` (after `DEFINE('FOO(X)')`)

### Pattern match

```
        SUBJ pattern... :S(YES) :F(NO)
LBL     SUBJ pattern... :S(YES) :F(NO)
```

A statement whose first token after the (optional) label is a
variable, immediately followed by another token (not `=`), is a
pattern match. The pattern is built from the parts listed in
[Pattern parts](#pattern-parts) below. Up to 8 parts per statement.

There is **no pattern-replacement assignment**. Standard SNOBOL4
allows `S pattern = replacement` to mutate the subject in place;
this interpreter only matches, never replaces. To rewrite a string
you must build a new one.

### Goto

Any statement may end with a goto suffix:

- `:(LBL)` -- unconditional jump
- `:S(LBL)` -- jump if the previous match (or test predicate)
  succeeded
- `:F(LBL)` -- jump if it failed

The `END` keyword on its own line marks the end of the program.

### REFLECT

```
        REFLECT VAR
LBL     REFLECT VAR
```

In-place pronoun swap on a string variable. See
[REFLECT](#reflect-pronoun-swap) below for the table of swaps.

### DUMP

```
        DUMP
```

Print the current values of all integer variables (debug aid).

## Pattern parts

| part            | meaning                                                |
|-----------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| `'literal'`     | match an exact substring                               |
| `SPAN(class)`   | consume a maximal run of chars drawn from `class`      |
| `BREAK(class)`  | consume up to (not including) any char in `class`      |
| `LEN(N)`        | consume exactly N chars (`N` may be int or variable)  |
| `REM`           | consume the rest of the subject                        |
| `var`           | match the run-time value of `var` as a literal         |
| `. VAR`         | capture the most recently matched span into `VAR`      |

`class` is a string literal whose characters form the alphabet for
that part. Pattern matching is unanchored: the engine tries each
starting position in the subject until one of them matches the
whole pattern, or every position has been tried (then it fails).

There is no `LEN(0)` issue; matching zero chars succeeds at the
current cursor.

Patterns currently **not** supported (would require interpreter
extension):

- `ANY(class)` (single char from a class)
- `NOTANY(class)`
- Pattern alternation `|`
- Conditional / immediate value assignment `$ VAR`
- Recursion via `*PATTERN`
- The `ARB`, `BAL`, `POS`, `RPOS`, `TAB`, `RTAB`, `SUCCEED`, `FAIL`
  primitives

## Builtins (predicate-style)

These return success or failure for `:S` / `:F` branching. They
are 2-arg only.

| builtin       | meaning                                |
|---------------|----------------------------------------|
| `IDENT(A,B)`  | succeeds if A and B are identical      |
| `DIFFER(A,B)` | succeeds if A and B differ             |
| `GT(A,B)`     | A > B                                  |
| `EQ(A,B)`     | A = B                                  |
| `LE(A,B)`     | A <= B                                 |

There is no `LT`, `GE`, or `NE`. Use `:F` on `LE`/`GT`/etc. to get
the opposite.

## Builtins (value-returning)

| builtin              | meaning                                  |
|----------------------|------------------------------------------|
| `REMDR(A,B)`         | integer A mod B                          |
| `ARRAY('lo:hi')`     | allocate a 1-D array                     |
| `SPAN(class)`        | (also a pattern part; assignable to var) |
| `BREAK(class)`       | (also a pattern part)                    |

`SPAN`/`BREAK` can be assigned to a variable to make a reusable
pattern: `DIGITS = SPAN('0123456789')`, then `S DIGITS . N`.

## REFLECT (pronoun swap)

The `REFLECT VAR` statement walks the string in `VAR` word by word
and rewrites each known word in place. Built for ELIZA-style
demos but useful anywhere you want first/second-person inversion.

| input    | output  |
|----------|---------|
| `I`      | `YOU`   |
| `ME`     | `YOU`   |
| `MY`     | `YOUR`  |
| `MINE`   | `YOURS` |
| `AM`     | `ARE`   |
| `YOU`    | `I`     |
| `YOUR`   | `MY`    |
| `YOURS`  | `MINE`  |
| `ARE`    | `AM`    |
| `WAS`    | `WERE`  |
| `WERE`   | `WAS`   |

Words are runs of non-space characters; matching is exact (the input
is already uppercased by `READ_INPUT`). Unrecognized words pass
through unchanged.

## DEFINE'd functions

```
        DEFINE('FOO(X)')
        ...
FOO     FOO = '...result...' :(RETURN)
```

A user function is declared with `DEFINE('NAME(PARAM)')`. The
interpreter currently supports **single-argument** user functions
only, and at most 4 of them per program. The function body starts
at the label whose name matches `NAME`. Inside the body the
function name is also a variable: assigning to it sets the return
value. Use `:(RETURN)` to return successfully or `:(FRETURN)` to
return as a failure.

The call stack is 16 frames deep, so recursion is allowed up to
that depth.

## I/O

- `OUTPUT = expr` prints `expr` followed by a newline. Concatenated
  expressions print as a single line.
- `LINE = INPUT` reads one line from the current input source:
  - Batch mode: data file loaded at memory `0x090000`. Use
    `./scripts/run-snobol4.sh prog.sno data.dat`.
  - Interactive mode: live UART RX (typed input). Use
    `./scripts/run-snobol4-tty.sh prog.sno`. Backspace and Ctrl-D
    work; `INPUT` fails at EOF.
- The interpreter chooses between batch and interactive at startup:
  if no data file is loaded (first byte at `0x090000` is null) it
  switches to TTY mode automatically.
- Both paths uppercase ASCII a-z to A-Z as the line is read. There
  is currently no way to preserve case.

## Limits

These are the static sizes you'll bump up against on a complex
program. Defined in `include/snoglob.msw` (and in `include/am.msw`
for opcode numbers). Bump them as needed and rebuild; nothing is
runtime-allocated.

| limit                       | value     | notes                          |
|-----------------------------|-----------|--------------------------------|
| Source buffer (`SRC`)       | 8192 B    | one program file               |
| String literal length       | 127       | per literal                    |
| Identifier length           | 7         | effective; longer = collision  |
| Statements (`STMAX`)        | 128       | each comment line uses 1 slot  |
| Labels (`LBLMAX`)           | 32        |                                |
| Symbol table (`SYMMAX`)     | 24        | named variables in scope       |
| String buffer (`SB`)        | 4096 B    | grows during execution         |
| Token buffer (`TB`)         | 128       | per-token scratch              |
| Eval stack (`ESTK`)         | 128       | expression evaluation          |
| Pattern parts per stmt      | 8         | `PP_TYP/PP_VAL` slots          |
| Concat parts per stmt       | 4         | `EP_TYP/EP_VAL` slots          |
| User functions (`FN_*`)     | 4         | total `DEFINE`'d               |
| Call stack (`CSTK_*`)       | 16        | recursion depth                |
| Arrays (`ARR_DATA`)         | 200 elem  | total across all `ARRAY()`     |
| Pattern stack (`PSTK`)      | 16        | depth during one match         |
| Array element capacity      | 50/array  | `ARR_DATA(arr_id*50 + idx)`    |

## What's missing vs. classic SNOBOL4

In rough priority order, things real SNOBOL4 has that this
interpreter does not. Some are easy adds, some would be substantial
work; treat this as a wish list.

- **Pattern-replacement assignment** (`S pat = repl`). The single
  biggest missing feature; lots of canonical SNOBOL4 idioms (string
  edit, tokenize-and-rebuild, recursive pattern application) need
  it.
- **Conditional/immediate value assignment** (`$ VAR` after a
  pattern part).
- **`ANY` / `NOTANY` / `ARB` / `POS` / `RPOS` / `TAB` / `RTAB`**
  pattern primitives.
- **Pattern alternation** (`|`).
- **Recursive patterns** (`*PATTERN`).
- **`TABLE`** (associative array). Only fixed-size integer-indexed
  `ARRAY` is supported.
- **Multi-arg user functions.** `DEFINE('FOO(X,Y)')` parses but only
  the first arg is wired up.
- **String operations.** No `SIZE`, `REVERSE`, `REPLACE`, `TRIM`,
  `DUPL`, `SUBSTR`, `CONVERT`. (`REVERSE` can be done in user
  code with `LEN`; see `demos/reverse.sno`.)
- **`&` system variables.** No `&ALPHABET`, `&LCASE`, `&UCASE`,
  `&ANCHOR`, `&TRIM`, etc.
- **Real numbers.** Integers only.
- **Indirect goto** `:($VAR)`.
- **Backtracking** within a single statement beyond what
  `PAT_MATCH2` already does.
- **No way to preserve input case.** `READ_INPUT` always
  uppercases.

## Demos and examples to read

- `examples/hello.sno` -- simplest possible
- `examples/pattern-tutorial.sno` -- guided tour of pattern parts
- `examples/array.sno` -- string-valued arrays
- `examples/dating.sno` -- `DEFINE`'d functions, BREAK + REM,
  data file input
- `demos/sieve.sno` -- arrays + arithmetic loop
- `demos/gcd.sno` -- `REMDR`, integer concat in OUTPUT
- `demos/reverse.sno` -- character reversal via `LEN(I) LEN(1) . C`
- `demos/nqueens.sno` -- backtracking via three "used" arrays
- `demos/eliza.sno` -- the big one: pattern matching, capture,
  `REFLECT`, history ring, response rotation

For tutorials and demos still to be written see
`docs/planned-demos.md`.

Tests and demos all green. Here's the state report.

# State of the SNOBOL4 interpreter

## Test results

| Suite | Result |
|---|---|
| `just test` (14 unit tests: am, lower, exec, amdump, pat, cursor, pmatch, descr, snolib, snolib2, alloc, lexer, parser, symtab + test-limits) | ✅ all pass, exit 0 |
| `just demos` (10 examples: hello, hello-goto, count, span, span-fail, multiply, concat, break, input, array) | ✅ all pass, exit 0 |
| `just build` (full interpreter from `src/snobol4.plsw`) | ✅ compiles to 10319 asm lines, runs |
| `bash scripts/build-modular.sh` (legacy modular build using old tracked files) | ✅ "up to date" cache hit — not exercised |

## Blockers (one critical, several stale-doc)

1. **🔴 CRITICAL: the working interpreter is uncommitted.** `src/snobol4.plsw` (1761 lines, 31 PROCs — the complete single-file interpreter that the `build` target points at and that every demo and `just build` actually compiles) is **untracked**. The HEAD `justfile` references it (`./scripts/build.sh ... src/snobol4.plsw`), so HEAD is broken without the working tree. A `git clean -fd` would erase the entire interpreter. **Commit `src/snobol4.plsw` immediately.**

2. **🟡 Stale tracked files.** `src/sno_main.plsw`, `src/sno_lex.plsw`, `src/sno_exec.plsw`, `src/sno_util.plsw` are still in git but no longer used by `just build`. Still referenced by:
   - `scripts/build-modular.sh` (the legacy modular pipeline)
   - `scripts/run-snobol4-tty.sh` (mtime check on `sno_main.plsw`)
   - `docs/quickstart.md` (line 130)

3. **🟡 `docs/language-reference.md` limits table is stale.** Says `STMAX=128, SYMMAX=24, LBL_MAX=32, ESTK=128, ARR=200`. Reality (from `include/snoglob.msw`):

| Limit | Doc says | Code says |
|---|---|---|
| `STMAX` | 128 | **256** |
| `SYMMAX` | 24 | **64** |
| `LBL_MAX` | 32 | **64** |
| `ESTK_DEPTH` | 128 | **256** |
| `ARR_POOL` | 200 | **400** |

4. **🟡 `sno_engine.plsw` + `snolib.plsw` are early-stage scaffolding** — they `%INCLUDE snoglob` (which the working `snobol4.plsw` doesn't), so the modular split was started but never finished or wired into the build. `sno_engine.plsw` has a dummy `MAIN`. They sit untracked alongside the working monolith.

## Needs from plsw (the underlying language compiler)

**Nothing urgent.** Recent plsw fixes already landed and unblocked features:
- plsw#30 — `%DEFINE` table headroom → recovered macro space
- plsw#31 — proper halt instruction → removed inline-ASM workaround (commits `e7257c3`/`71e3db6`)

Future asks (only if you want the listed missing features):
- **Heap allocation primitive** — needed for `TABLE` (associative arrays), pattern alternation backtracking state, multi-arg function frames. Right now everything is statically sized via `%DEFINE`.
- **Larger `%DEFINE` table** — if SYMMAX/STMAX/ARR_POOL keep growing, will eventually run into the same wall #30 fixed.

## What's actually in the language right now

### Statements
`assignment`, `pattern match` (no replacement), `goto :(L) :S(L) :F(L)`, `END`, `REFLECT VAR` (in-place pronoun swap), `DUMP` (debug print all int vars).

### Expressions
Int / string literals, var ref, `+ - *`, unary minus, **concat (≤4 parts)** mixing strings and ints, `INPUT`, `ARRAY('1:N')`, `arr<i>`, single-arg user function calls.

### Pattern parts (≤8 per stmt)
`'literal'`, `SPAN(class)`, `BREAK(class)`, `LEN(N)` (N int or var), `REM`, `var` (literal-of-runtime-value), `. VAR` (capture). `SPAN`/`BREAK` are also assignable to vars for reuse.

### Builtins
- **Predicate** (`:S` / `:F`): `IDENT`, `DIFFER`, `GT`, `EQ`, `LE`
- **Value-returning**: `REMDR(a,b)`, `SIZE(s)`, `SUBSTR(s,pos,len)`, `CHAR(code)`, `ARRAY('lo:hi')`

### I/O
`OUTPUT = expr` (newline appended). `LINE = INPUT` reads from data file (batch) or live UART (interactive); auto-detects which based on whether a data file is loaded at `0x090000`. **Both paths uppercase the input** (no way to preserve case currently).

### User functions
`DEFINE('FN(P)')`, single arg, body at label `FN`, `:(RETURN)`/`:(FRETURN)`. Max 4 functions, recursion depth 16.

### Limits (current, from `snoglob.msw`)

| | |
|---|---|
| Source buffer | 8 KB |
| String literal length | 127 |
| Identifier effective length | 7 |
| Statements | 256 |
| Symbols | 64 |
| Labels | 64 |
| String pool | 4 KB |
| Eval stack | 256 |
| AM bytecode buffer | 4 KB |
| Arrays | 8 × 50 elem = 400 pool |
| Pattern stack | 16 |
| User functions | 4 |
| Call stack | 16 |
| Token buffer | 128 |

## Could you write a Fortran compiler in this SNOBOL4?

Short answer: **a tiny FORTRAN-66 subset, yes; anything realistic, no — at least four hard blockers first**.

What would help you (the good news):
- Pattern matching with `SPAN`/`BREAK`/`LEN`/literal/capture is exactly the toolkit for tokenizing FORTRAN's fixed-column source.
- `ARRAY('1:N')` gives you a symbol table substrate.
- `REMDR`, `SIZE`, `SUBSTR`, `CHAR` cover the basic string surgery a code generator needs.
- `INPUT` with auto-uppercase aligns with FORTRAN's case-insensitive convention.
- `DEFINE`'d functions let you factor out passes.
- The 8 KB source buffer is enough for a single small FORTRAN file.

What would block you (the bad news, in order):

1. **No pattern-replacement assignment** (`S pat = repl`). The single canonical SNOBOL4 idiom for source rewriting / macro expansion / token stream editing isn't there. You'd build new strings explicitly with concat — workable but verbose, and concat is capped at 4 parts.
2. **No `TABLE`** (associative arrays). Symbol tables, intrinsic-name lookups, label tables — all the things FORTRAN compilers need a hash on — would have to be implemented as parallel `ARRAY` + linear scan. Slow and limited to the array-pool budget.
3. **No multi-arg user functions.** A code generator wants `EMIT(opcode, operand)` and `LOOKUP(name, scope)`. You'd thread the second arg through globals.
4. **64-symbol limit on the SNOBOL4 program itself.** Not the FORTRAN source — the compiler-as-a-SNOBOL4-program gets 64 named variables to hold all of its passes' state. Tight. Same for 256 statements in the compiler source.
5. **Minor:** no pattern alternation `|`, no `ANY`/`NOTANY`/`ARB`, no recursive patterns. The lexer for FORTRAN keywords would be a series of literal matches with `:F` chains, not one alternation.

**Recommendation if you actually want to do this:** add pattern-replacement (`S pat = repl`) and `TABLE` first. Those two unlock the canonical SNOBOL4 compiler-writing style; the rest you can work around.

## Recent additions (since the m5 saga, on `main`)

- **Builtins**: `SIZE`, `SUBSTR`, `CHAR` (issue #1)
- **Limits raised**: `SYMMAX 24→64`, `STMAX→256`, `LBL_MAX 32→64`, `ESTK→256`, `ARR_POOL 200→400`
- **Bugfix**: silent memory corruption from `VARS(24)` vs `SYMMAX=64` mismatch (issue #2/#3)
- **Bugfix**: `SPAN`/`BREAK` pattern vars no longer corrupt `REM` matching with arrays (issue #4)
- **Refactor**: executor uses `SELECT/WHEN` dispatch + grouped handler procedures
- **I/O**: live UART `INPUT` + batch INPUT both work, auto-detect at startup
- **Feature**: `REFLECT`, `DUMP` statements
- **Feature**: lifted string-literal cap to 127
- **Constants consolidated**: all sizes now `%DEFINE`'d in `include/snoglob.msw` (no more magic-number drift)
- **Build**: dependency tracking via `build/.build-deps` manifest in `scripts/build-modular.sh`
- **Demos added**: sieve, nqueens, eliza (+ enhancements), palindrome, fibonacci, factorial, gcd, reverse, plus tutorials

## Summary

**Healthy:** all tests pass, all demos pass, the language has matured noticeably since FizzBuzz — `SIZE`/`SUBSTR`/`CHAR`, larger limits, real `INPUT`, `REFLECT`, ELIZA-class demos work end-to-end.

**One urgent action:** **commit `src/snobol4.plsw`** (and decide what to do with `sno_engine.plsw` + `snolib.plsw` and the now-stale tracked `sno_*.plsw` files). The repo is one `git clean` away from losing the entire working interpreter.

**One housekeeping action:** refresh `docs/language-reference.md` limits table to match `snoglob.msw`.

**Strategic question for you:** is the `snobol4-runtime-split` saga still the right next move? The motivation was modularity for compiled-program output, but the working interpreter is currently a healthy monolith and the half-built `sno_engine`/`snolib` files have been dormant. Might be worth either (a) finishing the split, (b) deleting the dormant files and the legacy `sno_*.plsw`, or (c) parking the split and doing something else next (e.g., the FORTRAN-compiler-prerequisites: pattern replacement + TABLE).

message.content: Let's pick a direction here.  I want a modular SNOBOL4 compiler, not a monolith.  The development needs to clearly transition from one to the other, and continuing to use one large .plsw file is not wanted.  How do we clean things up so that there is an archived/referenced tagged "old" version and a working "new" version that is the only one getting features/fixtures going forward and make it very clear to all demos and to the fortran compiler project to only use the new interpreter.  How close are we to being able to do this?

content.text: Let me investigate concretely before recommending. Three candidates exist in some form — I need to know how complete each is.

content.content:
#!/bin/bash
# build-modular.sh -- Build SNOBOL4 interpreter from modular sources
#
# Pipeline (per docs/linker-design.md):
#   1. Check staleness via dep manifest; skip if all deps unchanged
#   2. Compile each .plsw module to .s (library modules use %DEFINE LIBRARY)
#   3. meta-gen prep: identify external refs, rewrite to la rN,0 placeholders
#   4. Pass 1 assembly: assemble at base 0 for sizes
#   5. Compute base addresses (entry module first)
#   6. Pass 2 assembly: reassemble with --base-addr
#   7. meta-gen emit: produce .meta from pass-1 .lst + .syms
#   8. link24: combine binaries, patch FIXUP references
#   9. Result: build/snobol4.bin
#
# Dependency tracking:
#   After a successful build, writes build/.build-deps containing the
#   mtime of every source and include file. On the next invocation,
#   if snobol4.bin exists and no dep is newer than the manifest,
#   the entire build is skipped. Pass -f to force a rebuild.

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_DIR="${SCRIPT_DIR}/.."
BUILD="$PROJECT_DIR/build"
INC="$PROJECT_DIR/include"
SRC="$PROJECT_DIR/src"

# Tools
LINKER_DIR="$HOME/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-plsw/components/linker/target/release"
LINK24="$LINKER_DIR/link24"
META_GEN="$LINKER_DIR/meta-gen"

INCLUDES="$INC/descr.msw $INC/heap.msw $INC/am.msw $INC/pat.msw $INC/snoglob.msw"

# Module order: entry module first, then libraries
ENTRY=sno_main
LIBS="sno_util sno_lex sno_exec"
ALL_MODULES="$ENTRY $LIBS"

DEPS_FILE="$BUILD/.build-deps"
FORCE=0
if [ "${1:-}" = "-f" ]; then
    FORCE=1
fi

# --- Staleness check ---
need_build() {
    if [ "$FORCE" -eq 1 ]; then return 0; fi
    if [ ! -f "$BUILD/snobol4.bin" ]; then return 0; fi
    if [ ! -f "$DEPS_FILE" ]; then return 0; fi
    # Check every source and include against the dep manifest mtime
    for MOD in $ALL_MODULES; do
        for f in $INCLUDES "$SRC/${MOD}.plsw"; do
            if [ ! -f "$f" ]; then
                echo "  Missing dep: $f" >&2
                return 0
            fi
            # Look up recorded mtime for this file
            rec=$(grep "^$(basename "$f")|" "$DEPS_FILE" 2>/dev/null | head -1 | cut -d'|' -f2)
            cur=$(stat -f%m "$f" 2>/dev/null || stat -c%Y "$f" 2>/dev/null)
            if [ -z "$rec" ] || [ "$cur" -ne "$rec" ]; then
                echo "  Stale: $f (recorded=$rec, current=$cur)" >&2
                return 0
            fi
        done
    done
    return 1
}

mkdir -p "$BUILD/mod"

if need_build; then
    echo "=== Building SNOBOL4 interpreter (modular) ===" >&2
else
    echo "=== SNOBOL4 interpreter up to date ===" >&2
    exit 0
fi

# --- Phase 1: Compile each .plsw to .s ---
for MOD in $ALL_MODULES; do
    echo "  [1] Compiling $MOD..." >&2
    "$PROJECT_DIR/scripts/build.sh" $INCLUDES "$SRC/${MOD}.plsw" >/dev/null 2>&1
    if [ ! -f "$BUILD/${MOD}.s" ]; then
        echo "  ERROR: $BUILD/${MOD}.s not produced" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    cp "$BUILD/${MOD}.s" "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}.s"
done

# --- Phase 2: meta-gen prep (rewrite external la refs to placeholders) ---
for MOD in $ALL_MODULES; do
    echo "  [2] meta-gen prep $MOD..." >&2
    "$META_GEN" prep "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}.s" \
        -o "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}_prep.s" \
        --syms "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}.syms"
done

# --- Phase 3: Pass 1 assembly (base 0 -> sizes + .lst) ---
SIZES=()
for MOD in $ALL_MODULES; do
    cor24-run --assemble "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}_prep.s" \
        "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}_p1.bin" "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}_p1.lst" >/dev/null 2>&1
    SZ=$(stat -f%z "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}_p1.bin" 2>/dev/null || stat -c%s "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}_p1.bin" 2>/dev/null)
    SIZES+=($SZ)
    echo "  [3] $MOD: $SZ bytes" >&2
done

# --- Phase 4: Compute base addresses ---
BASES=()
ADDR=0
MODS_ARR=($ALL_MODULES)
for i in $(seq 0 $((${#SIZES[@]} - 1))); do
    BASES+=($ADDR)
    ADDR=$((ADDR + ${SIZES[$i]}))
done
echo "  [4] Layout: $(for i in $(seq 0 $((${#SIZES[@]} - 1))); do printf "%s@0x%04X " "${MODS_ARR[$i]}" "${BASES[$i]}"; done)" >&2

# --- Phase 5: Pass 2 assembly (with --base-addr) ---
for i in $(seq 0 $((${#MODS_ARR[@]} - 1))); do
    MOD="${MODS_ARR[$i]}"
    cor24-run --assemble "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}_prep.s" \
        "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}.bin" "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}.lst" \
        --base-addr "${BASES[$i]}" >/dev/null 2>&1
done

# --- Phase 6: meta-gen emit (produce .meta from pass-1 .lst) ---
for MOD in $ALL_MODULES; do
    echo "  [6] meta-gen emit $MOD..." >&2
    "$META_GEN" emit "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}_p1.lst" \
        --syms "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}.syms" \
        --module "$MOD" \
        -o "$BUILD/mod/${MOD}.meta"
done

# --- Phase 7: link24 ---
echo "  [7] Linking..." >&2
"$LINK24" --entry "$ENTRY" --dir "$BUILD/mod" \
    --map "$BUILD/mod/snobol4.map" \
    $ALL_MODULES -o "$BUILD/snobol4.bin" 2>&1 | head -3 >&2

TOTAL=$(stat -f%z "$BUILD/snobol4.bin" 2>/dev/null || stat -c%s "$BUILD/snobol4.bin" 2>/dev/null)
echo "  Output: $TOTAL bytes -> build/snobol4.bin" >&2

# --- Write dep manifest ---
> "$DEPS_FILE"
for MOD in $ALL_MODULES; do
    for f in $INCLUDES "$SRC/${MOD}.plsw"; do
        mt=$(stat -f%m "$f" 2>/dev/null || stat -c%Y "$f" 2>/dev/null)
        echo "$(basename "$f")|$mt" >> "$DEPS_FILE"
    done
done

echo "=== Build complete ===" >&2

ARGS = INPUT :F(NOARGS)
        DMPLN = 0
        ARGS '-DUMP-LINES' :S(HASDMPL)
        :(RDLN)
HASDMPL  DMPLN = 1
RDLN     STL = ARRAY('1:30')
        STR = ARRAY('1:30')
        STLAB = ARRAY('1:30')
        STTXT = ARRAY('1:30')
        SP = SPAN(' ')
        DG = SPAN('0123456789')
        STCT = 0
        LINECT = 0
RLINE   LINECT = LINECT + 1
        GT(LINECT,100) :S(STOP)
        LINE = INPUT :F(RDONE)
        IDENT(LINE,'') :S(RLINE)
        C1 = ' '
        LINE LEN(1) . C1 REM . TAIL :F(RLINE)
        IDENT(C1,'C') :S(RLINE)
        IDENT(C1,'*') :S(RLINE)
        C6 = ' '
        REST = ''
        TAIL LEN(4) . DUM LEN(1) . C6 REM . REST :F(NOSIX)
        :(HAVECOL)
NOSIX   REST = TAIL
HAVECOL  DIFFER(C6,' ') :F(NEWST)
        GT(STCT,0) :F(NLERR)
        STR<STCT> = LINECT
        STTXT<STCT> = STTXT<STCT> ' ' REST :(RLINE)
NEWST   GT(STCT,0) :F(FIRST)
        STR<STCT> = LINECT

#!/bin/bash
# run-snobol4.sh -- Run a SNOBOL4 program on COR24
#
# Usage: ./scripts/run-snobol4.sh program.sno [data.dat]
#
# The interpreter is loaded from build/snobol4.bin (modular build).
# SNOBOL4 source at 0x080000, optional data at 0x090000.

set -uo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
INTERP_BIN="$PROJECT_DIR/build/snobol4.bin"

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <program.sno> [data.dat]" >&2
    echo "" >&2
    echo "Examples:" >&2
    for f in "$PROJECT_DIR/examples/"*.sno; do
        [ -f "$f" ] && echo "  $0 $(basename "$f")" >&2
    done
    exit 1
fi

SNO_FILE="$1"
if [ ! -f "$SNO_FILE" ]; then
    if [ -f "$PROJECT_DIR/examples/$SNO_FILE" ]; then
        SNO_FILE="$PROJECT_DIR/examples/$SNO_FILE"
    else
        echo "Error: $SNO_FILE not found" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
fi

DAT_FILE=""
if [ $# -ge 2 ]; then
    DAT_FILE="$2"
    if [ ! -f "$DAT_FILE" ]; then
        if [ -f "$PROJECT_DIR/examples/$DAT_FILE" ]; then
            DAT_FILE="$PROJECT_DIR/examples/$DAT_FILE"
---
#!/bin/bash
# build.sh -- Compile and run a SNOBOL4 .plsw program
#
# Usage: ./scripts/build.sh [include/*.msw ...] src/program.plsw
#
# Uses the PL/SW compiler from sw-cor24-plsw.
# Outputs assembly to build/<name>.s and runs it.

set -euo pipefail

PLSW_DIR="${PLSW_DIR:-$HOME/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-plsw}"
COMPILER_ASM="${COMPILER_ASM:-$PLSW_DIR/build/plsw.s}"

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 [include/*.msw ...] src/program.plsw" >&2
    exit 1
fi

if [ ! -f "$COMPILER_ASM" ]; then
    echo "Error: PL/SW compiler not found at $COMPILER_ASM" >&2
    echo "Build it: cd $PLSW_DIR && just build" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Separate .msw and .plsw files
MACROS=()
MAIN=""
for f in "$@"; do
    case "$f" in
        *.msw) MACROS+=("$f") ;;
        *.plsw) MAIN="$f" ;;
        *) echo "Error: unknown file type: $f" >&2; exit 1 ;;
    esac
done

if [ -z "$MAIN" ]; then
    echo "Error: no .plsw file specified" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Show registered includes

END_MARKER="--- end assembly ---"

content.text:
I owe you a major correction to my prior report. **You already have a modular SNOBOL4 interpreter.** I had it backwards.

## What's actually true

**The working interpreter is and has been modular.** It's the 4-file split — all tracked, all current:

| File | Role | LOC |
|---|---|---|
| `src/sno_main.plsw` | driver: `MAIN`, calls READ_SRC/PARSE/LOWER_ALL/AM_EXEC | 21 |
| `src/sno_util.plsw` | utilities: I/O, READ_SRC, READ_INPUT | 20 PROCs |
| `src/sno_lex.plsw` | lexer + parser + AM emit | 14 PROCs |
| `src/sno_exec.plsw` | lowering + executor + pattern matching + builtins | 26 PROCs |

Glued together by `include/snoglob.msw` (shared globals) and built by `scripts/build-modular.sh` into `build/snobol4.bin`. **Every demo, every tutorial, the TTY runner, and `scripts/run-snobol4.sh` all use this modular binary.** I just confirmed by running `examples/test_builtins.sno` through `run-snobol4.sh` — `SIZE(HELLO)=5`, `SUBSTR(ABC,1,1)=A`, `CHAR(65)=A`, all work.

## What I got wrong before

I saw `src/snobol4.plsw` (untracked, 1761 lines) being referenced by `just build`, ran `just build`, saw exit 0, and concluded it was the working interpreter. It is not. It's a **stale single-file copy** that:
- Predates the SIZE/SUBSTR/CHAR work (no `OP_SIZE`/`OE_SIZE` in it)
- Is referenced **only** by the `justfile build:` target — which compiles it and runs it as a standalone program (it has its own dummy MAIN that exits in 324 instructions)
- Is loaded by **zero** demos. `just demos` runs `scripts/run-snobol4.sh` which loads `build/snobol4.bin` from `build-modular.sh` — the legacy modular pipeline.
- Was never tracked in git. It's an abandoned experiment.

Same story for `src/sno_engine.plsw` + `src/snolib.plsw`: they're an unfinished **second** experimental refactor (a 3-module *runtime/engine* split — different shape from the working 4-module split). Never wired into either build, never tracked, dormant since Apr 10.

## How close are you to "modular only"

**You're already there architecturally.** The cleanup is hygiene — maybe 30 minutes of work — to remove confusion between the real modular setup and the dead untracked files.

## Proposed cleanup plan

**Phase A — delete dead code (all untracked, zero git impact):**
1. `rm src/snobol4.plsw` — the dead 1761-line monolith
2. `rm src/sno_engine.plsw` — the unfinished 3-module refactor draft
3. `rm src/snolib.plsw` — same

**Phase B — repair build entry points:**
4. Fix the `justfile build:` target. It currently points at the dead monolith. Replace with `bash scripts/build-modular.sh` (and `just build -f` for force-rebuild). This makes `just build` actually build the working modular interpreter.
5. Optionally: add `just clean` and `just rebuild` targets pointing at the modular pipeline.

**Phase C — make "modular only" explicit and irreversible:**
6. Update `CLAUDE.md` with a new section: **"The interpreter is modular. Edit `src/sno_main.plsw`, `src/sno_util.plsw`, `src/sno_lex.plsw`, `src/sno_exec.plsw`. Do NOT create monolithic single-file interpreter sources."** Belt-and-suspenders against an agent re-creating `snobol4.plsw`.
7. Add `src/snobol4.plsw` and `src/sno_engine.plsw` and `src/snolib.plsw` to `.gitignore` (or just `src/snobol4.plsw`) — so even if a future agent recreates them, they don't sneak into commits.
8. Update `docs/language-reference.md`:
   - Refresh the stale limits table (STMAX 128→256, SYMMAX 24→64, LBL 32→64, ESTK 128→256, ARR 200→400)
   - Replace "interpreter source lives in `src/sno_*.plsw`" with an explicit list of the four files and what each holds
9. Update `docs/quickstart.md` line 130 if the reference is stale.

**Phase D — tag a "before refactor" reference point:**
10. Tag the current commit (`6ad1719`) as `v0-modular-stable` so you have a known-good reference before the runtime-split saga begins. Then the next saga's first commit can be the boundary between "current modular" and "runtime-split modular".

**Phase E — frame the next saga properly:**
11. The `snobol4-runtime-split` saga, if you want to do it, becomes a *re-modularization* — splitting the existing 4 modules along a different axis (runtime helpers vs compiler engine vs driver) so that `snolib.plsw` can be linked into compiled SNOBOL4 programs without dragging in the lexer/parser/executor. This is a real architectural change with motivation: standalone compiled `.sno → .bin` output. It's not "make it modular" (already done), it's "split the modular boundary differently".

## How this affects the FORTRAN compiler

Once Phase A–C are done, the answer to "what should the FORTRAN compiler use?" is unambiguous: **the four `src/sno_*.plsw` modules + `include/snoglob.msw` + `include/*.msw`, built via `bash scripts/build-modular.sh`.** Same as every demo. There's no decision to make and no risk of pointing at the wrong interpreter.

## Open questions before I act

1. **OK to delete the three untracked files in Phase A?** They're completely safe (untracked, dormant, replaced) but I want explicit approval before `rm`.
2. **Phase B `justfile build:` change** — replace with a `build-modular.sh` call, or delete the target entirely?
3. **Phase D tag name** — `v0-modular-stable`? `pre-runtime-split`? Or pick your own?
4. **Do you want the `snobol4-runtime-split` saga to start right after this cleanup**, or pause it and do something else next (e.g., the FORTRAN-compiler prerequisites — pattern-replacement + TABLE)?

Tell me which phases to run and which questions to answer, and I'll execute. No actions taken yet.

Do not delete untracked files.    So tag the next (not current) commit to get an actual snapshot of now.  Then we can proceed through your recommended phases.  Phase B no need to say build-modular (should be implied by build), Phase 0 tag name "fallback pre-cleanup"; phase D tag name is fine.  start saga after cleanup.  fortran support will arrive when it arrives.  document all of this in docs/plan.md before committing anything.

1	# Plan -- SNOBOL4 on PL/SW for COR24
2	
3	## 1. Plan summary
4	
5	This plan sequences the work so that each phase:
6	
7	1. validates a risky architectural assumption early
8	2. produces tooling before complexity multiplies
9	3. keeps semantic and machine concerns separated
10	4. results in usable dogfooding infrastructure in PL/SW
11	
12	## 2. Development strategy
13	
14	The overall strategy is:
15	
16	1. build the runtime substrate first
17	2. build diagnostics immediately
18	3. bring up a small evaluator before broad syntax
19	4. add pattern execution as a first-class subsystem
20	5. lower into AM-oriented forms rather than hard-wiring direct tree walking forever
21	6. expand the runtime to support real programs before optimizing internals
22	7. delay GC and architectural cleanup until real workloads justify them
23	
24	## 3. Milestone sequence
25	
26	```
27	M0  Runtime substrate and diagnostics          [DONE]
28	M1  Minimal execution core                     [DONE]
29	M2  Abstract machine boundary                  [DONE]
30	M3  First pattern subsystem (SPAN, capture)    [DONE]
31	M4  Richer runtime (arrays, functions, I/O)    <-- next
32	M5  Dating app demo                            <-- target demo
33	M6  Pattern engine improvements (BREAK, etc.)
34	M7  Pattern lowering and graph form
35	M8  Garbage collection maturity
36	M9  Self-dogfooding improvement tools
37	```
38	
39	Rationale for resequencing (vs original plan):
40	
41	- M4 (richer runtime, was M6) moves up because real programs need
42	  arrays, functions, builtins, and I/O before the pattern engine needs
43	  refactoring. Features before architecture.
44	
45	- M5 (dating app) is the exit test for M4. It exercises parsing,
46	  arrays, functions, arithmetic, string matching, multi-pass
47	  processing, and I/O. See docs/student-dating-app.txt.
48	
49	- M6 (pattern improvements) adds BREAK, REM, and other primitives
50	  needed for the dating app's record parser. Could merge into M4 if
51	  small enough.
52	
53	- M7 (pattern graph form, was M4) deferred because the current
54	  pattern engine works. Refactor when there is something worth
55	  refactoring.
56	
57	- M8 (GC, was M5) deferred because short-lived demo programs do not
58	  need garbage collection. Only matters with long-running or
59	  loop-heavy workloads.
60	
61	- M9 (self-dogfooding, was M7) last, as before.
62	
63	## 4. Milestones
64	
65	## Milestone 0 -- runtime substrate and diagnostics [DONE]
66	
67	### Goals
68	
69	- Prove that PL/SW can comfortably host the low-level runtime substrate.
70	- Establish regular descriptor and heap object conventions.
71	- Build tooling before semantics grow.
72

186	
187	## Milestone 5 -- dating app demo
188	
189	### Goals
190	
191	- Run a complete, historically-inspired SNOBOL4 application.
192	- Exercise the full interpreter feature set.
193	- Produce demo-worthy output for blogging/vlogging.
194	
195	### Target program
196	
197	Late-70s style campus dating profile matcher (see docs/student-dating-app.txt):
198	
199	- reads pipe-delimited student records from a data file
200	- parses records using BREAK('|') and . capture
201	- stores fields in parallel arrays
202	- computes pairwise compatibility scores (major, interests, eye/hair)
203	- prints matches with contact info from a directory lookup table
204	- uses user-defined functions (HAS, COMMON, SCORE)

207	### Sample data
